text: The surgeon injects vasopressin mixed with normal saline into the dissection plane, preparing the area for laparoscopic myomectomy.
The surgeon performs hydro dissection to establish a clear plane around the myoma, ensuring that surrounding tissue and connective tissue are separated without damage to collateral tissues.
The surgeon separates the tissue, revealing the encapsulated myoma, which is clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon positions a myoma screw on the left side of the port and begins retracting the myoma, gradually pulling it out from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses two myoma screws to provide optimal bilateral symmetrical traction on the myoma. After securing the second screw, the first screw is withdrawn to adjust the traction.
The surgeon carefully separates the base of the myoma from surrounding structures, avoiding abrupt avulsion to prevent damage to vessels and ureters.
The myoma is visibly extracted from the surrounding tissue, facilitated by hydrodissection, revealing its encapsulated structure.
The surgeon prepares to use a uterine manipulator and a morselator to morselate the myoma, a process that involves gradually fragmenting the myoma tissue.
The surgeon uses a morselator to gradually remove the myoma in small pieces, facilitating its extraction from the surgical site.
